meta.name: Doro Wat (Ethiopian Chicken Stew)
meta.subtitle: Berbere-forward wot with slow-cooked onions, niter kibbeh, and eggs (optional).

source.name: SSA Canonical Catalog
notes: Canonical Ethiopian doro wat pattern: onion reduction + berbere bloom + long simmer.
Eggs are traditional but treated as optional for flexibility.

summary: Deeply savory, spicy Ethiopian chicken stew built on long-cooked onions and berbere, traditionally finished with eggs and served with injera.

yield.notes: Serves ~6 with injera or rice; ~8 if served as part of a platter with multiple sides.

optional.label: Food processor (for onions)
optional.notes: Optional; makes fine onion mince faster.

notes: Contains dairy if using niter kibbeh, butter, or ghee (swap to neutral oil for dairy-free).

ingredients.label: Chicken thighs and/or drumsticks
notes: Bone-in, skin-on is traditional; skinless also works.
Cut large thighs in half for faster simmer and more surface area.

ingredients.label: Lemon juice
ingredients.notes: Used to brighten and lightly tenderize chicken; substitute vinegar if needed.

ingredients.label: Yellow onions, very finely minced
notes: Doro wat uses a large onion base; mince very fine to reduce smoothly.
If using a processor: pulse to mince, do not puree.

ingredients.label: Niter kibbeh (spiced clarified butter) or ghee
notes: Substitute: 60 g ghee + 15 ml neutral oil, or 60–75 ml neutral oil for dairy-free.

ingredients.label: Berbere spice blend
notes: Start at 24 g for medium heat; 36–48 g for hot, depending on blend.
Add in stages so it blooms without scorching.

ingredients.label: Tomato paste
ingredients.notes: Optional but common; deepens color and savory notes.

ingredients.label: Chicken stock or water
ingredients.notes: Use water for a cleaner berbere-forward profile; stock for richer body.

ingredients.label: Hard-boiled eggs, peeled
ingredients.notes: Traditional finish; add near the end so they warm through and absorb sauce.

notes: Key success factor: patiently reduce onions until jammy before adding berbere and liquid.
Keep heat moderate once spices are in—berbere can scorch quickly.
For a thicker sauce: reduce uncovered for the last 10–15 minutes.
For a smoother sauce: onions must be finely minced; avoid large chunks.

changes.berbere: Use 18–24 g berbere; add 2–3 g paprika for color without heat.

changes.fat: Replace niter kibbeh/ghee with 60–75 ml neutral oil.
changes.note: Flavor will be slightly less buttery but still authentic in profile.

changes.protein: Use boneless thighs cut into large chunks; simmer 25–35 minutes after adding chicken.

notes: Cool leftovers quickly and refrigerate within 2 hours.

reheatNotes: Reheat gently; add a splash of water if sauce thickens.
Bring to a simmer to ensure safe reheating.

freezer.notes: Freeze sauce + chicken together; eggs are best added fresh after thaw/reheat.

leftoversPlan: Day 2: Reheat doro wat; add quick greens side.
Day 3: Serve remaining sauce over rice; add fresh tomato salad.

steps.title: Season chicken
instructions: Pat chicken dry.
Season with the salt and toss with lemon juice.
Set aside while you start the onion base (10–20 minutes is fine).
checks: Chicken is evenly salted and lightly coated with lemon.

steps.title: Start onion reduction (dry then fat)
instructions: Add minced onions to a heavy pot over medium heat.
Cook, stirring often, until moisture releases and onions begin to soften and reduce (they should look like a thick mash, not raw chunks).
If onions start sticking early, add 2–3 tbsp water and scrape fond; continue reducing.
checks: Onions have reduced significantly and are soft, not browned.

steps.title: Add niter kibbeh/ghee and continue reducing
instructions: Add niter kibbeh (or ghee) to the onions.
Cook on medium-low, stirring frequently, until onions turn jammy and aromatic and most moisture is cooked off.
You’re building the sauce body here—be patient.
checks: Onion base is thick, glossy, and reduced; no harsh raw onion smell.

steps.title: Bloom aromatics and berbere (do not scorch)
instructions: Stir in garlic and ginger; cook 30–60 seconds until fragrant.
Add berbere in 2 additions, stirring constantly to bloom in the fat.
If using tomato paste, add it now and cook 1–2 minutes to deepen color.
If pan looks dry, add a splash of water to prevent scorching.
checks: Spices smell toasted and fragrant, not burnt.

steps.title: Add liquid and simmer to form sauce
instructions: Add chicken stock (or water) gradually while stirring to create a smooth sauce.
Bring to a gentle simmer.
Simmer 15–20 minutes, stirring occasionally, until sauce thickens slightly.
checks: Sauce is cohesive and lightly thickened; oil may begin to separate slightly at the edges.

steps.title: Simmer chicken until tender
instructions: Add chicken pieces to the simmering sauce, turning to coat.
Cover partially and simmer gently until chicken is cooked through and tender, about 35–50 minutes depending on cut.
Stir occasionally and adjust with a splash of water if sauce becomes too thick.
checks: Chicken is tender and reaches 165°F / 74°C in the thickest part.

steps.title: Finish with eggs (optional) and final seasoning
instructions: If using eggs, add peeled hard-boiled eggs to the pot and spoon sauce over them.
Simmer 5–8 minutes to warm through and let eggs absorb sauce.
Taste and adjust salt. Add a small splash of lemon juice if you want a brighter finish.
checks: Sauce is balanced; eggs are warmed through (if used).

steps.title: Serve
instructions: Serve hot with injera (traditional) or rice (fallback).
For platter service, add sides like gomen (greens), atkilt wat (cabbage-potato-carrot), and timatem salad.
checks: Stew is hot, sauce is thick enough to cling, and serving base is ready.

change.notes: Maintains bloom step; flavor shifts slightly less buttery.
Reduce berbere to 18–24 g; keep paprika for color.
Rice is a common fallback base when injera is unavailable.
